title: Weight on Other Planets Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Weight on Other Planets
description: Find out how much you'd weigh on the Moon, Mars, Jupiter, and every planet. Free calculator, no signup required.
seoKeywords: how much would you weigh on mars
how much would you weigh on jupiter
intro: Your **weight** changes based on the planet's gravity. On the Moon, you'd weigh only **16.5%** of what you weigh on Earth, while on Jupiter you'd weigh **2.53 times more**. This calculator shows your weight on every body in our solar system.
keyTakeaway: **Weight = mass × gravity** → If you weigh **70 kg** on Earth: **11.6 kg on the Moon**, **26.5 kg on Mars**, **177 kg on Jupiter**.
useCases: Find out how much you'd weigh on the Moon or Mars.
Compare gravity across all planets in our solar system.
Understand why astronauts bounce on the Moon.
Visualize gravitational differences across the solar system.

faq.q: Why does my weight change on different planets?
faq.a: Your **mass** stays constant, but your **weight** = mass × local gravity. Each planet has different surface gravity.
**1.62 m/s²** (16.5% of Earth's). A 70 kg person weighs 11.6 kg on the Moon.

faq.q: Which planet would I weigh the most on?
faq.a: **Jupiter**: g = 24.79 m/s² (2.53× Earth's). You'd weigh 2.53 times more. On the Sun's surface, 28× more.

faq.q: Which planet has the least gravity?
faq.a: Among planets, **Mars** (0.38g). Among all bodies, small asteroids or Pluto (0.06g).

faq.q: Does my mass change on other planets?
faq.a: **No.** Mass is the amount of matter and never changes. What changes is your **weight** (gravitational force).

faq.q: How much do astronauts weigh on the ISS?
faq.a: ~**0 kg** (microgravity). Not because there's no gravity, but because the station and astronauts fall together (free fall).

faq.q: Why do astronauts jump so high on the Moon?
faq.a: Lower gravity (16.5% of Earth's) means less downward force, so your jump height multiplies by about 6×.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator for exoplanets?
faq.a: If you know the exoplanet's surface gravity, yes. Multiply your mass by that gravity value.

explanation:
## Why Your Weight Changes on Each Planet

Your **mass** (the amount of matter that makes you up) is constant throughout the universe. What changes is your **weight**, which is the force of gravity pulling on you: **W = m · g**. Each planet has its own gravitational acceleration (g).

## Gravity and Weight Factor Table

| Body | g (m/s²) | Factor vs Earth |
|---|---|---|
| Moon | 1.62 | 0.165 |
| Mercury | 3.70 | 0.378 |
| Venus | 8.87 | 0.907 |
| Mars | 3.71 | 0.379 |
| Jupiter | 24.79 | 2.528 |
| Saturn | 10.44 | 1.065 |
| Uranus | 8.87 | 0.904 |
| Neptune | 11.15 | 1.137 |
| Sun | 274 | 27.9 |
| Pluto | 0.62 | 0.063 |

## How the Calculation Works

**Weight on planet X = Weight on Earth × (gX / 9.81)**. If you weigh 70 kg on Earth, on Mars you'd weigh 70 × (3.71/9.81) = 26.5 kg.

## When to Use This and Common Mistakes

- **"Weight in kg" is technically inaccurate**: the correct unit is Newtons. But in everyday language, kg is understood.
- **Jupiter has no solid surface**: the gravity listed is at "high cloud level."
- **Saturn weighs almost as much as Earth**: its low density compensates for its massive size.
- For the physics behind this, explore [hydrostatic pressure](/hydrostatic-pressure) and [simple pendulum period](/simple-pendulum-period).

example.title: Real-world example: 70 kg visitor
steps: **Data**: weight on Earth = 70 kg, g Earth = 9.81 m/s².
**Mars**: 70 × 0.379 = **26.5 kg** (lighter but harder to move around).
example.result: At 70 kg on Earth, you'd weigh 12 kg on the Moon, 27 kg on Mars, and 177 kg on Jupiter.
